The computational model for studying tritium behavior at steady state provides for variation of the metal permeability coefficients of the steam-system tubes, but assumes that diffusion through the tube walls varies only with the $\frac{1}{2}$ power of hydrogen partial pressure.

Tritium distribution calculations were made for a variety of situations in which it was assumed that the effective permeabilities of the oxide coatings in the steam system were $1, 10^{-1}, 10^{-2}$ , and $10^{-3}$ times those of the bare metal at a hydrogen partial pressure of 1 torr $(130\mathrm{Pa})$ .
These results were compared with cases without oxide coatings in which the permeabilities of the bare metal were reduced by factors of 1, 10, $10^{2}$ , and $10^{3}$ .

The comparisons were made at three values of the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio $(10^{2}, 10^{3}$ , and $10^{4}$ ) and, in all cases, sorption of hydrogen or HF on core graphite was assumed to be negligible.
The results (Table 1.1) indicate that a low-permeability oxide coating would be more effective than a low permeability in the metal itself for limiting tritium transport to the steam system.

(The total resistance to tritium transport was not doubled because of the contribution from the salt film.) The results with a factor of $10^{3}$ reduction in a steam-tube permeability due to oxide formation indicate that tritium transport to the steam system could be limited to the design objective of 2 Ci/day.

The hourly drift would be equivalent to a change in bubble-separator efficiency of about $10\%$ during salt operation (assuming a void fraction of $0.3\%$ at the inlet to the bubble separator).

Modifications to the salt cold trap (SCT) were completed, and the loop was started up on March 14, 1975, and operated for 1279 hr to check the effectiveness of the salt mist filter, to obtain data on salt mist generation rates under different operating conditions, and to obtain salt and off-gas sample data in preparation for the tritium tests.
The SCT flow lines were disconnected from the system, and the loop was started up on March 14, 1975.
The loop operated continuously until May 6, 1975, when it was shut down to permit installation of equipment in the containment enclosure for the tritium tests.

The loop was started again on June 27, 1975, and it was still in operation at the end of the report period, when more than $2500\mathrm{hr}$ of operating time had been logged without plugging in the off-gas line.
This is convincing evidence that the salt mist filter has been effective, since the off-gas line had plugged after only $240\mathrm{hr}$ of operation before the mist filter was installed.3
The loop is operating at a pump speed of 1790 rpm (estimated salt flow rate, 54 liters/sec) and a pump bowl
gas overpressure of $2.67 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~Pa}$ (2000 mm Hg abs).
The pump bowl off-gas flow, which consists of helium containing a few percent of $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ , plus trace quantities of condensible material, is about 2 liters/min (STP).
The $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ concentration of the off-gas is a function of the $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ partial pressure in the salt, which in turn is a strong function of the salt temperature.

Except for short periods of time when special tests required a different setting, the salt circulating temperature has been maintained at 535 to $540^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ , at which point the $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ concentration in the off-gas stream is about $2.5\%$ by volume.
The loop off-gas stream, except for a $100-\mathrm{cm}^{3}/\mathrm{min}$ sample stream, is passed through a $-72^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ cold trap (dry ice-alcohol bath).

Material which is a dirty white solid at trap temperature and a dirty brown fluid upon warming to room temperature, and which is rich in tritium (about $10^{6} \mathrm{nCi/g}$ ), continues to collect in the cold trap at a rate of 1 to $10 \mathrm{ng/cm}^{3}$ .

At this time, the loop has operated approximately 3000 hr at design $\Delta T$ conditions with the expected low corrosion rates.
Loop FCL-2b was operated continuously for about 3000 hr from February to June 1975, under design $\Delta T$ (565°C minimum, 705°C maximum) conditions.
During this period, standard Hastelloy N corrosion specimens installed in the loop in January 1975 were exposed to circulating fuel salt at three different temperatures, (565, 635, and 705°C).
As expected, corrosion rates were low; the highest value was 0.1 mil/year (3μm/year) at the highest temperature station.
Salt samples taken at intervals have been analyzed for major constituents, metallic impurities, and oxygen (Table 2.4).
Analytical probe readings for the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio, indicative of the redox condition of the salt, have been taken on a weekly basis.
This ratio, which was about $7\times 10^{3}$ at the beginning of the corrosion run, rapidly dropped to about $1\times 10^{3}$ after the first 24 hr of operation.
The ratio then gradually fell to $\sim 1\times 10^{2}$ by the end of March ( $\sim 1500$ hr elapsed time), and it has remained at that level during the latter part of the operation.

Additional samples of condensate collected during the operation of the Coolant Salt Test Facility (CSTF) were analyzed (Table 4.1).

The condensates show a tritium concentration factor of about $10^5$ relative to the salt, suggesting that fluoroborate coolant salt $[\mathrm{NaBF}_4\text{-NaF} (92 - 8\text{mole}\%)]$ may be an effective means of concentrating and conveying tritium out of the system.

Attempts were made to generate a condensable fraction in laboratory-scale experiments by heating coolant salt containing up to $200\mathrm{ppmH}$ as $\mathrm{NaBF}_3\mathrm{OH}$ to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in a closed system equipped with a cold finger.

The $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ concentration in the salt decreased to $50~\mathrm{ppm}$ , and the composition of the condensate in a typical run was $53.2\%$ $\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{OBF}_4$ , $14.8\%$ $(\mathrm{H}_3\mathrm{O})_2\mathrm{SiF}_6$ and $32\%$ free water found by difference.

An apparatus was assembled to measure the vapor density of $\mathrm{BF}_3\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ and related compounds at elevated temperatures to determine the degree of dissociation of these materials.

This work tested the hypothesis that the condensable materials collected in the operation of the CSTF are completely dissociated at operating temperatures $(400 - 600^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ and only combine to form more complex molecules in the colder parts of the system.

The procedure consists in measuring the pressure developed in a closed system containing a known amount of $\mathrm{BF}_3\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ or DHFBA in order to establish the degree of dissociation according to the equilibrium described below:
Initial runs with $\mathrm{BF}_3\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ indicate that this compound may be completely dissociated at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Work on determining the oxide species present in molten fluoroborate is being continued, and the survey<sup>1</sup> of the system $\mathrm{NaF - NaBF_4 - B_2O_3}$ at 400 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ has been extended to include IR and x-ray diffraction analyses in addition to physical and chemical observations of the behavior of selected compositions.
This corresponds to mixtures containing more than 33 mole $\% \mathrm{B}_2\mathrm{O}_3$

Usually coolant salt $\left[\mathrm{NaBF}_4\text{-NaF}\right.$ (92-8 mole $\%$ ) contains relatively small amounts of oxide, up to 1000 ppm, and its composition lies within area 3; thus, work has been directed toward characterizing the oxide species in this area.

At least two species were present; one formed at the boundary of the glass area (high oxide content), and the other was $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{B}_3\mathrm{F}_6\mathrm{O}_3$ , which formed in compositions having NaF: $\mathrm{NaBF}_4:\mathrm{B}_2\mathrm{O}_3$ mole ratios of 2:2:1 and 2:4:1 and was possibly present in
compositions containing as little as 3 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{O}_3$ .
Experiments at the 1.5 to 4.0 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{O}_3$ level, approaching the coolant composition, have been impeded by the relatively low sensitivity of IR and x-ray powder diffraction.

The difficulty with IR, using the KBr pellet method, arises from the fact that, at these oxide levels, the only band not covered by $\mathbf{BF}_4^-$ absorptions is the one at $810~\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ .

A more certain IR identification can be made only when at least two typical bands can be identified (presently observable only at higher concentrations), as was the case in the identification of $\mathbf{Na}_3\mathbf{B}_3\mathbf{F}_6\mathbf{O}_3$ at an oxide level corresponding to 14 mole $\%$ $\mathbf{B}_2\mathbf{O}_3$ .

A modified Hastelloy N containing $2\%$ Ti has good resistance to irradiation embrittlement; however, it remains to be shown that the alloy has sufficient resistance to shallow intergranular cracking.

Since the solubility of graphite in bismuth-lithium solutions appears to increase with increasing lithium concentration, a molybdenum thermal-convection loop that contained graphite specimens was run to study mass transfer in a Bi-2.5% Li solution.

An alloy that contains $2\%$ Ti appears to adequately resist irradiation embrittlement, but it remains to be demonstrated that this alloy satisfactorily resists shallow intergranular attack by the fission product tellurium.
Increasing the chromium concentration from the present $7\%$ to a value in the range of 12 to $15\%$ may also be beneficial in preventing shallow intergranular attack.

Currently, factors associated with production of the $2\%$ Ti-modified alloy in commercial quantities are being studied, while smaller heats are being made of Hastelloy N containing both $2\%$ Ti and additions of niobium and rare earths.
Two large heats, one 10,000 lb and the other 8000 lb, of the $2\%$ Ti-modified alloy have been melted by a commercial vendor.
Tests are being run in which specimens are exposed at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to the low partial pressure of tellurium vapor in equilibrium with tellurium metal at $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Three materials (standard Hastelloy N, Inconel 601, and type 304 stainless steel) were exposed in this manner during the first TeGen experiment, and their cracking tendencies closely parallel those noted in laboratory tests in which these materials were exposed to tellurium vapor.
Fuel pins for a second experiment have been filled with salt containing $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and will be irradiated in the near future.

6.31, the 503 specimen given the standard 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal demonstrated good creep rupture life, while the 114 specimen given the same treatment had a comparably short lifetime.

Therefore, the response of titanium-modified Hastelloy to solution annealing at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was studied as a function of time at temperature.
Samples of alloy 451 $(0.017\%)$ were annealed in argon at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for $15\mathrm{min}$ to $8\mathrm{hr}$ .
The samples were cleaned electrochemically for $6\mathrm{hr}$ to remove any surface effects, and the carbides were electrochemically extracted, separated, and weighed.
Only extremely small amounts of precipitates were present in samples annealed for $2\mathrm{hr}$ or more.
At times less than $2\mathrm{hr}$ , there was some scatter in the data, but, in general, slightly more precipitate was extracted.
These results indicate that 30 to $60\mathrm{min}$ are needed in addition to the standard 1-hr solution anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to dissolve the carbides completely.
